section.title: Preach the Word
section.sectionRationale: The letter reaches its charge. Before God and Christ the Judge, Paul solemnly commands Timothy to preach the word, ready in season and out, precisely because a time is coming when people will not endure sound doctrine but will gather teachers to suit their own desires. Then Paul turns to himself: already being poured out like a drink offering, he looks back on a finished race and forward to the crown of righteousness, held not for him alone but for all who love Christ's appearing.
section.thesis: In view of Christ's coming judgment and kingdom, Timothy must preach the word faithfully in every season, even as people turn from sound doctrine to myths; for Paul, poured out and near departure, has fought the good fight and kept the faith, and the righteous Judge will award the crown to all who love His appearing.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the solemn charge to preach the word in season and out of season; the warning that people will not endure sound doctrine, so Timothy must fulfill his ministry; and Paul poured out like a drink offering, finishing the race and awaiting the crown of righteousness.

verses.text: I {{diamartyromai|charge}} you in the presence of God and of Christ Jesus, who will judge the living and the dead, and in view of His {{epiphaneia|appearing}} and His kingdom:
{{kerysso|Preach}} the word; be prepared {{eukairos_akairos|in season and out of season}}; reprove, rebuke, and encourage with every form of patient instruction.
For the time will come when men will not tolerate {{hygiaino|sound}} doctrine, but with itching ears they will gather around themselves teachers to suit their own desires.
So they will turn their ears away from the truth and turn aside to myths.
But you, be sober in all things, endure hardship, do the work of an evangelist, fulfill your ministry.

units.label: Poured out, finishing well
units.note: the good fight, the crown of righteousness

verses.text: For I am already being {{spendo|poured out}} like a drink offering, and the time of my {{analysis|departure}} is at hand.
I have fought the {{agon|good fight}}, I have finished the {{dromos|race}}, I have kept the faith.

From now on there is laid up for me the {{stephanos|crown}} of righteousness, which the Lord, the righteous Judge, will award to me on that day, and not only to me, but to all who crave His appearing.

diamartyromai.gloss: To charge solemnly, to testify earnestly, to adjure as before a witness. Paul does not merely advise Timothy; he places him under oath in the presence of God and of Christ the Judge. The weight of the command comes from the One before whom it is given, so preaching the word is not one option among many but a sacred duty owed to God.

epiphaneia.gloss: A shining forth, an appearing, used for the visible coming of Christ in glory. The charge is framed by His appearing and His kingdom, so all ministry is done in the light of Christ's return and reign. The same appearing that Timothy is to serve is the appearing that Paul, at the end, longs for and loves.

kerysso.gloss: To herald, to proclaim publicly as an official messenger. A herald does not invent his message but announces the word given by his king. So Timothy is to preach the word, not his own opinions, delivering God's message with the authority of the One who sent him and adding nothing and subtracting nothing.

eukairos_akairos.gloss: In season and out of season, when it is convenient and when it is not. The paired adverbs press the herald to constant readiness: he does not wait for a welcome or a favorable mood but stands prepared at every moment. Faithfulness in preaching is measured not by the audience's appetite but by the messenger's readiness.

hygiaino.gloss: To be healthy, sound, in good health; the root of our word hygiene. Sound doctrine is healthy teaching that produces spiritual health, in contrast to teaching that corrupts. The tragedy Paul foresees is people rejecting what heals them in favor of what merely flatters, choosing sickness because it suits their desires.

spendo.gloss: To be poured out as a drink offering, a libation poured beside a sacrifice. Paul sees his coming death as an act of worship: his life's blood poured out to God like the wine poured over an altar. His martyrdom is not defeat but offering, the fitting end of a life already given wholly to Christ.

analysis.gloss: A loosing, an unbinding, a departure; the word for casting off a ship's moorings or striking camp to set out. Paul does not speak of dying so much as of departing, of being released to go home. Death for the believer is the loosing of ropes for a voyage to the Lord, not an end but a setting out.

agon.gloss: A contest, struggle, or fight, drawn from the athletic games and the battlefield. The Christian life is no gentle stroll but a strenuous contest that demands everything. Paul does not say he won by his own strength but that he fought the good fight, the noble contest worth all its cost, staying in it to the end.

dromos.gloss: A course, a running track, the race set before a runner. Paul pictures his ministry as a marked-out course now completed, not abandoned partway. To finish the race is to persevere to the appointed end, and the joy is not in speed or applause but in having run the whole distance God assigned.

stephanos.gloss: A crown, the victor's wreath awarded to the winner of the games, not a royal diadem. The crown of righteousness is the reward of grace given by the righteous Judge, laid up and certain. It is promised not to Paul alone but to all who love Christ's appearing, so the finish line and its crown belong to every faithful believer.

basic.context: Having grounded Timothy in the God-breathed Scriptures, Paul now issues the charge the whole letter has been building toward. He commands Timothy, as if under oath before God and Christ the Judge, to preach the word at all times, whether it is welcomed or not. He warns that people will increasingly refuse healthy teaching and collect teachers who tell them what they want to hear. Against that tide Timothy must keep a clear head, bear hardship, and finish his ministry. Then Paul speaks of himself: his death is near, his life is being poured out like an offering, but he has stayed faithful to the end and a crown awaits him and everyone who loves Christ's return.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a direct charge to a pastor, yet its truths reach the whole church. Preaching God's word faithfully, whether or not it is popular, is the God-appointed means of guarding the flock. The warning about itching ears is a permanent danger, not a one-time event. And Paul's confidence at death models the hope held out to every believer who keeps the faith.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul solemnly charges, diamartyromai, Timothy in view of Christ's epiphaneia, His appearing. He is to kerysso, to herald, the word eukairos akairos, in season and out of season, because people will refuse hygiaino teaching, sound and healthy doctrine. Paul is being spendo, poured out like a drink offering, near his analysis, his departure. He has fought the agon, the good fight, finished the dromos, the race, and awaits the stephanos, the victor's crown.
basic.typology: The drink offering poured beside the sacrifices of Israel is fulfilled as Paul pours out his life to God. The victor's crown of the games becomes the crown of righteousness given by the righteous Judge to all who finish faithful.
basic.christ: Christ is the Judge of the living and the dead, the King whose appearing and kingdom frame the whole charge. He is the righteous Judge who awards the crown, and the One whose appearing believers are to love. The word Timothy preaches is the message of Christ, and the faith Paul kept is faith in Him.
basic.god: God, before whom the charge is given, will judge all people through Christ. He is the God to whom Paul's life is poured out as an offering, and the faithful Judge who has laid up a sure reward. He is worthy of a ministry that does not bend to the crowd but holds to His word to the end.
basic.crossReferences: Philippians 2 speaks of Paul being poured out as a drink offering on the sacrifice of their faith. First Corinthians 9 pictures the Christian life as a race run for an imperishable crown. Second Timothy 3 grounds this charge in the God-breathed Scriptures Timothy is to preach.
basic.oneStory: God has always advanced His purposes by His word carried on faithful lips, from prophets to apostles to preachers like Timothy. Paul, at the end of his course, hands the word on and pours out his life, confident that the same word will run on after him. The story moves toward Christ's appearing, when the righteous Judge will crown all who kept the faith and loved His coming.

context.literary: The movement opens with a formal, oath-like charge, I charge you in the presence of God, and drives to a string of sharp imperatives: preach, be prepared, reprove, rebuke, encourage. The reason follows in verses 3 and 4, the coming refusal of sound doctrine, answered by the fivefold command of verse 5. Then the tone shifts from command to testimony as Paul speaks of his own departure, moving from the athlete's finished race to the crown laid up for all who love Christ's appearing.
context.historical: Paul writes as a prisoner in Rome expecting execution, most likely under Nero. The drink offering and the language of departure fit a man who knows his death is imminent. Timothy pastors in Ephesus amid pressure to soften the message, and the itching ears Paul describes match a culture eager for teachers who confirm its desires. The victor's crown would call to mind the wreaths awarded at the Greek games familiar to Timothy's world.
context.canonical: These are among the last recorded words of the apostle Paul, and verses 7 and 8 have become the church's classic testimony of a faithful death. The charge to preach the word in season and out of season stands as the enduring commission of Christian ministry, and the crown for all who love His appearing extends Paul's hope to every believer.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lay upon Timothy, with the full weight of God's presence and Christ's judgment, the abiding duty of faithful preaching, and to strengthen him by the example of Paul's own faithful, hope-filled death, so that the word would be guarded and passed on when Paul was gone.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The commands to preach, endure, and fulfill one's ministry are prescriptive, binding on Christian ministry in every age. Paul's account of his imminent death and finished race is descriptive testimony, yet the crown promised to all who love Christ's appearing turns his personal hope into a promise held out to the whole church.

debates.question: Is the charge to preach the word only for pastors, or for the whole church?

views.label: Primarily the pastoral office
views.case: Paul addresses Timothy, a minister, and the terms preach and fulfill your ministry describe the formal task of the herald entrusted with the public teaching of the church.

views.label: A pattern touching every believer
views.case: While the office of preaching is specific, the call to hold and speak God's word faithfully, in season and out, applies in its measure to all who confess Christ.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage exalts the faithful proclamation of God's word as the appointed means of guarding the church against the drift into myths.

debates.question: What is the crown of righteousness Paul expects?

views.label: The reward of a righteous life crowned by grace
views.case: The crown is given by the righteous Judge to those who have kept the faith, a gracious reward at the end of a faithful course.

views.label: The crown that consists of righteousness itself
views.case: The phrase can mean the crown which is righteousness, the perfected righteousness believers receive fully at Christ's appearing.

debates.bearing: Both readings rest on grace, not merit, and both are secured by the righteous Judge and promised to all who love Christ's appearing.

study.originalLanguages: The charge carries courtroom weight: Paul says diamartyromai, I solemnly charge you, before God and Christ, and in view of the epiphaneia, the appearing of Christ in glory. Timothy is to kerysso, to act as a herald who proclaims the king's word rather than his own, and to do so eukairos akairos, in season and out of season, ready when it is welcome and when it is not. The reason is that people will not endure hygiaino teaching, doctrine that is healthy and gives health, preferring teachers who suit their itching ears. Paul then speaks of his death in worship language: he is being spendo, poured out like a drink offering, and the hour of his analysis, his departure, the loosing of a ship's moorings, has come. He has contended in the agon, the noble contest, finished the dromos, the marked-out race, and there is laid up for him the stephanos, the victor's wreath, the crown of righteousness the righteous Judge will award to all who love Christ's appearing.

typology.type: The drink offering poured beside the sacrifice
typology.fulfillment: Paul's life poured out to God in martyrdom as an act of worship, fulfilling the pattern of the libation in Israel's offerings.

typology.type: The victor's crown of the games
typology.fulfillment: The crown of righteousness awarded by the righteous Judge to all who finish the race of faith, at Christ's appearing.

study.christ: Christ stands at the center of the charge as the coming Judge of the living and the dead, whose appearing and kingdom set the horizon for all faithful ministry. The word Timothy is to preach is the gospel of Christ, and the sound doctrine people will refuse is the truth about Him. At the end Paul's hope is entirely in Christ: it is the Lord, the righteous Judge, who will award the crown, and it is His appearing that Paul, and every believer, is to love. The finished race and the poured-out life are the fruit of a whole life bound to Christ, and the crown is His gift of grace, not a wage earned.
study.god: God is the One in whose presence the charge is given and who will judge all people through Christ, so ministry is carried out as before His face. He is the God to whom Paul's life is offered like a drink offering, receiving the whole of a life spent in His service. And He is the faithful and righteous Judge who has already laid up the crown, so that the believer's future is not in doubt but secured by God's own justice and grace. Such a God is worthy of preaching that will not trim the message to please the crowd.

crossReferences.note: Paul speaks of being poured out as a drink offering on the sacrifice of their faith, the same image of a life offered to God.
The Christian life as a race run and a contest fought for an imperishable crown, requiring discipline to the end.
The athlete is not crowned unless he competes according to the rules, anticipating the crown of righteousness here.
When the Chief Shepherd appears, faithful shepherds will receive the unfading crown of glory, echoing the crown for all who love His appearing.

study.oneStory: God has carried His purposes forward by His word spoken through faithful servants, and here the aging apostle hands that word to the next generation before he departs. Paul's poured-out life and finished race are one link in a long chain of faithfulness that runs from the prophets, through the apostles, to every preacher who guards and proclaims the gospel. The whole story leans toward Christ's appearing, when the righteous Judge will crown all who kept the faith and loved His coming, and the word Paul suffered for will be vindicated forever.

sermon.title: Preach the Word
sermon.why: Part of Begg's series A Study in 2 Timothy, this message expounds the solemn charge of 2 Timothy 4:1-2, unfolding what it means to preach the word in season and out of season before God and Christ the Judge, and why faithful proclamation is the church's abiding duty.

quiz.q: On what basis does Paul issue his charge to preach the word?

options.text: In the presence of God and Christ the Judge, in view of His appearing and kingdom.
options.note: Verse 1 grounds the charge in God's presence and Christ's coming judgment and reign.

options.text: On the strength of Paul's own personal authority alone.
options.note: Paul writes with apostolic authority, but the charge is grounded in God and Christ the Judge, not himself.

options.text: Because preaching was culturally respected in that day.
options.note: Paul warns the culture will reject sound teaching; the ground is God, not popularity.

options.text: Because ministry is done before God and in light of Christ's return.
options.note: True and close, restating the coming appearing and kingdom that frame the charge.

quiz.q: What does in season and out of season require of Timothy?

options.text: Constant readiness to preach whether it is welcome or not.
options.note: The paired words press unwavering readiness in every circumstance.

options.text: Preaching only when the audience is eager to listen.
options.note: The opposite: he must preach even when it is unwelcome.

options.text: Preaching only during the appointed seasons of the year.
options.note: It is about readiness at all times, not the calendar.

options.text: Faithfulness that does not depend on the crowd's appetite.
options.note: True and close, capturing the readiness that ignores convenience.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say a time is coming when people will not tolerate sound doctrine?

options.text: Because with itching ears they will gather teachers to suit their own desires.
options.note: Verses 3 and 4 name itching ears and self-chosen teachers turning to myths.

options.text: Because sound doctrine will no longer exist to be preached.
options.note: The word remains; the problem is people refusing it.

options.text: Because all teachers will have abandoned the truth entirely.
options.note: The issue is hearers collecting agreeable teachers, not the total loss of faithful ones.

options.text: Because people will prefer teaching that flatters their desires.
options.note: True and close, summarizing the itching ears that reject healthy doctrine.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe his approaching death?

options.text: As being poured out like a drink offering, the time of his departure at hand.
options.note: Verse 6 uses the drink offering and departure to picture death as worship and release.

options.text: As a tragic and meaningless end to his work.
options.note: Paul sees it as offering and departure, full of hope, not meaningless.

options.text: As proof that his ministry had finally failed.
options.note: He speaks of a finished race and a crown, not failure.

options.text: As a life offered to God and a release to go home.
options.note: True and close, drawing out the offering and the departure imagery.

quiz.q: To whom is the crown of righteousness promised?

options.text: To Paul and to all who love Christ's appearing.
options.note: Verse 8 extends the crown beyond Paul to all who long for Christ's coming.

options.text: To Paul alone, as a unique apostle.
options.note: Paul explicitly says not only to me but to all who love His appearing.

options.text: To those who earn it by outdoing others in good works.
options.note: The crown is grace from the righteous Judge, not a reward for outperforming others.

options.text: To every believer who keeps the faith and longs for His return.
options.note: True and close, restating the promise to all who love His appearing.

questions: Paul charges Timothy before God and Christ the Judge. How would remembering that you live and serve in God's presence change the way you speak and act this week?
Faithful preaching means the word in season and out of season, welcome or not. Where are you tempted to soften or stay silent about God's truth because it is unpopular?
Itching ears gather teachers who confirm what they already want. What voices are you drawn to, and are they feeding you healthy doctrine or simply flattering your desires?
Paul faced death as a life poured out and a departure to be with the Lord. How does his hope reshape the way you think about your own death and what you are living for now?
The crown of righteousness is promised to all who love Christ's appearing. Do you long for His return, and how would loving His appearing change your priorities today?

translationNotes.note: In season and out of season renders two paired adverbs pressing constant readiness, whether the moment is convenient or not.
Poured out like a drink offering echoes the libation poured beside Israel's sacrifices; departure translates a word for loosing a ship's moorings or striking a tent.
The BSB uses a dash before and not only to me; this text renders the break with a comma, with no change to the wording. Crown here is the victor's wreath of the games, not a royal diadem.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Timothy 4:1-8. The only dash in the source, in verse 8, was replaced with a comma to comply with the no-dash rule; wording is otherwise unchanged.
